sentences.text: Good morning, Rita dear.
My name is Emil, E-M-I-L, Goldbarten, G-O-L-D-B-A-R-T-E-N.
I reside-- you want to-- where I reside, in North Miami Beach, 2801 NE 183rd Street, Apartment 1208, North Miami Beach, Florida, 33-- zip code 33160.
I was born in Komarow, in Poland.
K-O-M-A-R-O-W.
I was born July the 15th, 1906.
No.
My father-- my father was working about-- over somewhere else.
It was about over 200 miles, he was-- he was working.
When the first war broke out, our-- our-- our town was not far from the Austria, Russian-- Poland in that time didn't belong to Russia, was not far.
And my father was a-- was afraid we shouldn't-- we shouldn't get lost over there, or get tied up over there when the Austria will take this place.
So he sent people and he brought us to the-- not far where he was working.
The town was called Mizoch-- M-I-Z-O-C-H.
He brought us over there.
And-- and-- and over there since then, we stayed over there till the Holocaust.
I don't know, exactly.
I think it's about 4,000 or 5,000 people.
I don't know.
I didn't-- I didn't-- I know only one thing.
The Gentiles in our town, there were no more than about 200.
So-- so approximately the whole town was 99% Jewish.
Jewish.
It so happened after the First World War, after they gave this part of Ukraine, they gave it to Poland.
Our town, economically, the people lived very well, economically.
We were six, three brothers and three sisters.
Yeah, my-- the oldest brother was Dovid, David.
The second next to him was Simha.
And then came my first sister, Miriam.
And after my first sister, I came.
And after me came another sister, Baila.
And then came the youngest sister, Haya.
My father's name was Haim, and my mother's name was Bina.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
I knew-- I knew the anti-- the anti-Semitism in Poland exists, just the part where we were-- it did belong-- the part what it did belong to Poland, they were 10 million people.
They were 18 and a half million Ukrainian, a million Jews, and half a million Poles.
So the anti-Semitism, we didn't feel over there, because-- because the-- the majority of the-- they were Ukrainian.
And they didn't-- they didn't like the Poles.
So-- so we didn't-- we didn't feel it.
I didn't feel it.
Could you tell me a little bit how you celebrated your Jewish life, or how did you live your--
It so happened our town was very, very active.
We were very active in the Zionist movement.
So-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so it was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we-- we were-- how to say it-- we were Jewish nationalists.
We were-- we-- The whole youth was organized in youth Zionist movement, because they were Betar, they were Hashomer Hatzair, Hashomer [NON-ENGLISH].
All those youth organization from different parties, they were in our town.
And many young people from our town went to-- they went in that time to Palestine as halozim, pioneers.
And it so happened, my younger daughter-- my younger sister, Haya, she went-- she went to Palestine as a haluza.
I cannot recall exactly the year.
And it so happened, it's destiny.
And it so happened when something is destined to happen, it so-- it so happened one went away.
You could go to Israel two ways-- either as a pioneer, a haluza, or as a capitalist.
A capitalist has to have $5,000 in that time.
So one-- not far from-- from our house, a man-- a neighbor when-- decided to go to Israel as a capitalist.
And he was short, so-- so I gave him $2,000.
After later, when he will get the money back from the bank when he deposited, he should give it to my sister.
And when-- when he saw my sister-- it so-- it so happens she was a-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] she was a maid.
She was-- she was a maid.
So he brought the letter that she looks bad, she works hard.
And this was 1939.
So my mother did insist she should come for six months to rest.
So she came in for Passover, 1939.
It was in April, anyway, she came.
And when the Second World War broke out, after the-- after the pact between Hitler and Stalin, we were not far from the Soviet Union border.
So the first-- they came the other way to our-- to-- to our town.
And we became automatically, because Stalin, the father Stalin liberated us.
And we became automatically Soviet citizens.
And Palestine was also like here.
You have to be over there five years to become a Palestinian citizen.
And my daughter-- an my sister was only, I guess, four years, four years or three years.
I don't-- I don't know exactly.
So she didn't-- she wasn't a Palestine citizen.
And that's why she became also automatically a Soviet citizen.
I did everything what I could, and they didn't let her out.
And she perished, and Hitler killed her.
I live-- When I left Komarow, where I was born, it was in 1914.
I was in that time eight years old, eight years old.
And since-- since then I lived all that time in Mizocz.
Really-- really-- really, where I did grow up, I did in Mizocz.
What do you mean, whose home?

Oh, oh, we were in that time-- my older brother-- my older brother, in between time, he married his first cousin, and he lived in [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], he lived over there with his-- with his family.
And-- and the rest, all of us, my older brother with his family, with three children, he lived with us, because we built a two-story home.
And they were-- in-- in this home, there were two apartments we rented out.
In one apartment lived my-- my older-- the-- the second brother, he lived with his family with three children.
And in one apartment lived my younger-- the next to me, my sister Baila, with her-- with her husband and one child they have.
His name was Yankel, the boy name.
I wasn't married.
I was single.
I lived, and my parents were-- were alive.
We lived all together, except my older brother, who was-- it's approximately where-- where he lived from-- from Mizocz probably 230 or 40 miles.
I didn't know the real English term.
In Polish or Russian, they called them a [NON-ENGLISH]-- my father was a wood specialist.
What do you mean?
They came-- they were big companies who bought forest from the barons, from the big landowners.
They didn't buy the land.
There were just the trees on the land.
And they had to exploit it to make different kinds from these trees.

My father could give-- he could-- let's say if the company that he worked for them, if they told him here is 20 acres or 50 acres of forest, give me an estimate how much this approximately can bring so we will know how much to offer.
He could-- he was so good, he-- he could give you an estimate 10% more or 10% less.
And besides this, he was-- when they chopped the trees, they made different kind of things.
The most of them, they made from oak trees they made-- they called this, I don't know in English, the English term, they called this [NON-ENGLISH].
They made, with them-- for the barrels to age the scotch, scotch.
And they made different kind-- different kind of things for buildings and other things they made.
And he was the one who-- who watched they should do the right-- the right thing.
And did the same thing, and he paid the-- the workers.
This was his job.

Before-- before the second-- before the First World War, he used to come home only twice a year for Passover holidays and for-- and for this, I guess, Rosh Hashanah, and I mean, New Year's and-- and sukkot [BACKGROUND NOISE] he used to stay-- I don't-- I was a child.
I don't remember exactly.
Used to stay three weeks, or four weeks or five weeks, and he went-- and he went back.
And later on, when we moved during the-- the First World War, when we moved to Mizocz, so we were a family together.
Oh, where?
Oh, yes.
My father was over there-- very, very active.
My father was over there.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Not really a cantor, just he use-- I don't know if you know what it means, the meaning [NON-ENGLISH].
They are-- when you-- when you're [NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH], you have two parts, the first and the second.
My father used to blow the [NON-ENGLISH].
My father used  to daven over there, [NON-ENGLISH] always.
Not-- not [NON-ENGLISH].
My father was a-- a religious man.
Just in the same time he wasn't fanatic.
He wasn't, he wasn't fanatic.
My father was, not because he's my father.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He-- [CRYING] he was-- he was a wise man.
When-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When people-- when people do you know how he is in business, they had some kind of misunderstanding, they used to come to my father.
They call this a [NON-ENGLISH].
They used to come, and he was the one they used to come to he should straighten them out.
And with--
My mother was-- doing-- doing-- it was-- this is-- this is about-- so let's say '14, it's about, let's say 80 years ago-- my mother was a plain Jewish girl.
They didn't send them in that time to school.
My father-- my father was different.
They didn't send her to school.
She was just brought up to be able to daven, to pray in the temple, in the shul.
And that's all.
And to raise-- And to raise children.
And to raise-- to raise children.
So my father was different.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So let's say-- he didn't go, like I went to school.
He already didn't go, just because of his ability.
He picked up, he picked up.
If he would go to school, maybe-- maybe he would be-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a scientist.
Just-- just-- he picked up, and he wasn't-- the main thing he wasn't fanatic.
How far did you go in school?
And where did you go?
First of all, there was a law.
When we worked for the Russians there was a school.
They called this [NON-ENGLISH], means they were four classes more than a public school.
When you finish this, you could go to the-- to the sixth grade in high school, in high school.
So I went to this school.
And later on, later on, when-- when we became-- when we became-- I was brought up in the Ukrainian and the Russian language, in their cultures.
Later on, when we became part of Poland, so my father sent me about 240 miles to Chelm.
Over there, went to-- over there was-- was Jewish Polish high school.
Over there I went to high school, and I didn't go no further.
Yeah, I didn't go no further.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
In Poland, yeah.
You had to wear-- and even-- even in the public school, you had to wear a uniform.
And we were over there.
We-- we used to go six days-- Saturday, also to school.
And you used to go from 8 o'clock till 2 or 3 o'clock we used to go.
I don't even-- if you want me, I can't tell you what happened all the time.
I can tell you only one thing.
During 1918, the war between Poland and the Soviet Union.
So there was a famous [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a famous general.
I don't know if you've heard of him.
Budyonny.
He's-- he's-- they were a cavalry.
When they came in into our town, I remember like today.
I was wearing the hat, was a high one, the same like the army had.
And-- and they were-- and they were robbing the town.
So I jump out from the window, and I lost my hat-- I hit the window.
I lost my hat, and the rest I don't remember the uniform.
OK.
The hat I do remember, because of this episode.
No, this was in Mizocz.
In Mizocz.
This was in Mizocz.
From my-- from my-- from my young year as a child, till up to eight years, I do remember very, very little.
I don't remember.
We speak-- where, here?
Over there-- over there we spoke Yiddish.
Just after we moved to Mizocz, the youth, the Jewish youth, like my generation-- my generation, we spoke with ourselves Russian.
Yes.
The whole youth in our town was organized in Zionist.
They wasn't-- I-- I can say, I don't know exactly.
Maybe all of them.
What?
First of all we-- we learned Hebrew.

We were singing, we were dancing, and we were talking about-- about we will build, we will-- we will-- we will build Israel for the-- for the future generations, so-- so they will not, they will not suffer from anti-Semitism, from persecution, like the Jewish people did.
I did belong-- I was the organizer.
And I was-- they called this in Hebrew [HEBREW].
I was from the Jabotinsky's revisionism.
And I also-- this was considered the more elderly.
And for the young, we-- we made-- there was a Betar.
And my younger sister did belong to Betar.
And before-- before going to Israel, she had to go-- I don't know if you know the meaning, they call this [HEBREW], because we-- because she never did physical work.
So they had to prepare her.
She had to go away for a year or two.
They sent her far away.
And she had to-- to do physical work.
Not-- she-- she was even a maid in a Jewish house.
She did physical work.
She had to get prepared herself, what she will do in Palestine.
They called this [HEBREW], preparedness.
In Mizocz.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I want to give you-- you see, I told you about the house what-- what-- what we built.
And when my youngest sister next to me got married, we rented land from the Polish government.
Do you know in Europe, only the big towns-- only the big cities had electricity.
The small towns, they-- they-- they didn't have.
They-- they lit their houses with kerosene lamps.
So-- so I built the house over there, and I-- and I brought a big tank.
And we used to bring kerosene in cisterns.
And we used to sell-- sell them to the whole stores, to the-- to the-- to the stores, we used to be-- we used to sell them.
And they used to sell them to the customer.
So this date, my brother-in-law from my-- from my sister, he was over there-- over there, the manager.
